summary: The Docker-based reproduction confirms that an unauthenticated attacker can create a file, write a GIF89a-prefixed PHP polyglot payload through the elFinder connector, rename the file to .php, and trigger remote code execution by requesting it over HTTP.
expected_security_impact: Running the reproduction pipeline should cause the crafted polyglot file to be written through the elFinder connector, renamed to .php, and then accessed over HTTP, producing the string EVOHUNT_RCE_SUCCESS followed by the output of the id command inside the container.
actual_observed_result: Running ./reproduction/run.sh cloned the target repository, built the Docker image, started Apache with PHP and elFinder, executed the exploit chain (open → mkfile → put with GIF89a polyglot → rename to .php → HTTP GET), and received the EVOHUNT_RCE_SUCCESS proof string together with the uid/gid output of id in the HTTP response body.
verification_method: The exploit script checks the HTTP response body for the exact proof string EVOHUNT_RCE_SUCCESS. This distinguishes genuine PHP code execution from a generic server error, a static-file response, or an unrelated crash. The id command output following the proof string further confirms that OS-level command execution occurred.
evidence_produced_by_run: The elFinder open command returned the root directory hash without any authentication challenge.
The mkfile command created evohunt.txt in the volume root; the connector returned the new file hash.
The put command wrote the GIF89a-prefixed PHP payload to the file; finfo detected the content as image/gif (allowed MIME), so the MIME gate passed.
The rename command renamed evohunt.txt to evohunt.php; the name-only MIME gate resolved .php to text/plain (allowed), so the rename succeeded.
An HTTP GET to /files/evohunt.php returned GIF89a followed by EVOHUNT_RCE_SUCCESS:uid=33(www-data) gid=33(www-data) groups=33(www-data), confirming PHP code execution as the web-server user.

reproduction.source_acquisition: run.sh clones the target repository on the host, checks out the pinned vulnerable ref, and mounts that checkout into the container.
